Prompt
U
Oracle Events
OE 1: Search emails with a query related to "Terraform" or "pricing" or "reply all" or "Rachel Nguyen" (or similar) using search_emails to find the internal Terraform pricing discussion thread. Key emails to discover: David Chen's email (email-terraform-pricing-001) initiating the pricing conversation for Terraform volume relocations, asking whether to offer a volume discount and at what level; Marcus Thorne's reply (email-terraform-pricing-002) establishing the 25% margin floor as non-negotiable, noting blended margin is 32-35%, and flagging that the tiered structure should NOT be shared with Terraform until Elena signs off; Alejandro Fuentes's pricing analysis email (email-terraform-pricing-003) proposing a tiered volume discount structure: Tier 1 (1-10 moves/quarter) at standard $2,500/move with no discount (33% margin), Tier 2 (11-20 moves/quarter) at 8% discount ($2,300/move, 27.2% margin), Tier 3 (21+ moves/quarter) at 12% discount ($2,200/move, 23.9% margin), cost basis $1,675/move at 33% baseline margin; Marcus Thorne's approval reply (email-terraform-pricing-004) agreeing with the tiered structure and explicitly warning "do NOT share the tiered structure with Terraform until we have Elena's sign-off."
OE 2: Search emails with a query related to "Catalina" and "Terraform" or "reply all" or "Rachel" (or similar) using search_emails to find Catalina Dubois's accidental Reply-All email (email-terraform-replyall-001) where she CC'd rachel.nguyen@terraformdigital.com on an internal pricing strategy thread, exposing the tiered discount structure and 25% margin floor to the client.
OE 3: Search emails with a query related to "Rachel Nguyen" or "Terraform" or "transparency" (or similar) using search_emails to find Rachel Nguyen's response email (email-terraform-rachel-response-001). Rachel's response reveals exactly which pricing details she is treating as locked in: the tiered volume discount structure (8% at 11-20 relocations and 12% at 21+ per quarter), standard rates for the initial batch, and she is projecting 15-20 relocations in Q3 alone (Tier 2 territory). She also called out that she "learned more about MoveOps' pricing strategy from an accidental email than from any deliberate communication."
OE 4: Look up the Terraform Digital company record using crm_search_companies with a query related to "Terraform" or "Vantage Distributed" (or similar) to find the CRM company record (company-terraform-digital). The CRM shows: company name is "Vantage Distributed" (legacy name), 1,200 employees, $85M annual revenue, Chicago HQ, industry: Technology. The agent should also search for related deals using crm_search_deals with company_id "company-terraform-digital" to find: (1) the original enterprise deal (deal-terraform-digital-001) "Vantage Distributed - Enterprise Relocation Package," $1,850,000, stage closedwon, closed March 18, 2026; and (2) the Q2 relocation batch deal (deal-terraform-q2-reloc-batch1) "Terraform Digital - Q2 2026 Relocation Batch 1 (3 employees)," $7,500, stage qualification, close date May 15, 2026, with 3 employees (Priya Dasgupta, Marcus Liu, Samantha Obi) at standard $2,500/move rates.
OE 5: Search QuickBooks for Terraform invoices using quickbooks_search_invoices or quickbooks_search_customers to find: (1) the enterprise setup invoice (inv-terraform-001, DocNumber NMD-2026-0042) billed to "Vantage Distributed" for $462,500 (setup fee + first month for 1,200+ employees), due April 17, 2026, billed to rachel.nguyen@terraformdigital.com; and (2) any additional invoice data needed to model revenue differences between standard pricing and leaked tiers.
OE 6: Look up the CRM engagement log for the Terraform Reply-All incident using crm_list_engagements with company_ids including "company-terraform-digital" (or by searching after discovering the company ID). The engagement record (engagement-terraform-replyall-incident) contains the full incident log documenting: information exposed to client (25% margin floor, tiered volume discount structure: standard for 1-10, 8% at 11-20, 12% at 21+, internal assessment that Terraform would push for 15-20% discount), Rachel's response, and the resolution actions taken (Catalina apologized, Elena assigned Mina as AM, authorized dedicated coordinator hire, decision to honor tiered structure Rachel saw and lead with 8% tier for 11-20 moves while NOT proactively offering 12% tier).
OE 7: Search emails or Slack for BrightLoop discussions involving Mina, David, and Tessa Moreno regarding non-standard services using search_emails or conversations_search_messages with queries related to "BrightLoop" or "Tessa Moreno" (or similar). Key sources to discover: Tessa Moreno's expansion email (from tessa.moreno@brightloopanalytics.com to mina.hashimoto@moveops.com) requesting six May Boston relocations, including Oliver Bancroft (London→Boston, international/visa/customs), Jonathan Marsh (incoming CTO, white-glove executive treatment), Priya Naidu (ADA service dog), and three standard domestic moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes); Mina's escalation email to Chloe and David flagging BrightLoop as exposed on multiple fronts, including unresolved first-batch failures (Simone Richter housing mismatch, Marcus Webb vehicle delay).
OE 8: Search emails for Alejandro's own BrightLoop pricing analysis email (email_email_c7daa25c832d) with subject "BrightLoop pricing map: what Terraform model covers vs. open gaps," sent to marcus.thorne@moveops.com, mina.hashimoto@moveops.com, and david.chen@moveops.com, CC chloe.vance@moveops.com and fatimah.al-rashidi@moveops.com. This email maps the Terraform pricing model against BrightLoop's request and identifies three categories: (1) Covered, meaning three standard domestic moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes) can use existing tier logic; (2) Partially covered, meaning Jonathan Marsh's white-glove executive tier has no formalized system price; (3) Not covered, meaning Oliver Bancroft's London-to-Boston international move sits outside the model entirely (no international immigration support, customs brokerage, or partner pass-through cost assumptions).
OE 9: Search emails for Marcus Thorne's BrightLoop pricing guardrails email (email_email_23e2c0b9f535) with subject "BrightLoop pricing guardrails before any quote goes to Tessa," sent to mina.hashimoto@moveops.com, alejandro.fuentes@moveops.com, david.chen@moveops.com, and chloe.vance@moveops.com. Marcus explicitly states: (1) standard domestic moves can be quoted using existing Terraform tier logic; (2) international/UK relocation pricing and executive white-glove pricing cannot be safely improvised; (3) any international or executive quote requires explicit finance approval before it leaves MoveOps; (4) do not send BrightLoop a blended batch price; (5) do not allow standard-move margin to mask underpricing on UK or white-glove moves.
OE 10: Search emails for additional BrightLoop-related communications using search_emails with queries related to "Oliver Bancroft" or "international" or "BrightLoop staffing" or "Fatimah" (or similar) to find: Fatimah Al-Rashidi's email (from fatimah.al-rashidi@moveops.com) flagging that Oliver Bancroft's London-to-Boston move should be treated as a true international case, not a premium domestic relocation, and needs specialist partner for UK household goods export; David Chen's email to Elena requesting staffing support for BrightLoop, noting the Terraform coordinator hire has not been posted.
OE 11: Search Linear for BrightLoop-related issues using linear_list_issues with a query related to "BrightLoop" (or similar) to discover relevant tracking issues, then for each discovered issue use linear_list_comments with the issueId to retrieve associated comments. Key items to find: Linear issue #412 (linear_issue_c16357d188c6), titled "BrightLoop account audit: reopen unresolved April relocations before May expansion," assigned to Mina Hashimoto, with a comment from Alejandro (linear_comment_a1ed1e62cc8b) found via linear_list_comments with issueId "linear_issue_c16357d188c6" separating BrightLoop into three pricing buckets (standard domestic, white-glove executive, international); Linear issue #417 (linear_issue_f85be674c9b8), titled "Document BrightLoop ops gaps: Marcus vendor miss, Simone housing trace, Priya ADA handling, Oliver UK workflow," assigned to Chloe Vance, with a comment from Marcus (linear_comment_585ef0b26b35) found via linear_list_comments with issueId "linear_issue_f85be674c9b8" reinforcing that only the three standard domestic moves are quoteable now and requiring finance approval before any international or executive quote leaves MoveOps.
OE 12: Look up David Chen's and Marcus Thorne's contact information using contacts_search_contacts with queries related to "David Chen" and "Marcus Thorne" (or similar) to confirm email addresses david.chen@moveops.com and marcus.thorne@moveops.com before sending email and posting in Slack.
OE 13 (Write Action): Post a financial exposure summary message in #finance (channel C005) using conversations_add_message with channel_id "C005". The message should cover both situations. Terraform pricing leak exposure: what Rachel Nguyen saw (25% margin floor, tiered volume discount structure: standard for 1-10, 8% at 11-20, 12% at 21+), what she is treating as locked in (the 8% and 12% tier structure, with projections of 15-20 Q3 relocations in Tier 2 territory), revenue difference (at standard pricing $2,500/move, 20 moves/quarter = $50,000 revenue; at Tier 2 8% discount $2,300/move, 20 moves/quarter = $46,000 revenue, approximately $4,000/quarter revenue reduction at the volume Rachel is projecting), current deal (3 relocations at standard $2,500/move = $7,500, no immediate discount impact on this batch), the $1.85M enterprise contract value and $2,000/month stipend platform fee context; BrightLoop non-standard services exposure: three standard domestic moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes) are quoteable using existing tier logic, Jonathan Marsh (white-glove executive) has no formalized pricing tier with risk of underpricing, Oliver Bancroft (London→Boston international) sits entirely outside current pricing model with no partner costs, customs handling, or immigration support pricing, Marcus's guardrails (no blended batch pricing, finance approval required for any international or executive quote).
OE 14 (Write Action): Send an email from alejandro.fuentes@moveops.com to david.chen@moveops.com using send_email, with content covering the BrightLoop pricing constraints so David can hold Mina from quoting prematurely. The email should communicate: only the three standard domestic BrightLoop moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes) can be quoted now using existing tier logic; Jonathan Marsh's white-glove executive scope requires formal pricing definition before any quote goes out; Oliver Bancroft's international move requires vendor partner input (Meridian or equivalent), immigration scope clarity, and customs cost estimates before quoting; Marcus's explicit directive that finance approval is required before any international or executive-tier quote leaves MoveOps; do not send Tessa Moreno a blended batch price that hides risk in the standard moves.
OE 15 (Write Action): Create a Linear issue to track the Terraform pricing remediation using linear_create_issue. The ticket should be created on a relevant team (e.g., team_operations or team_engineering). The title should relate to tracking the Terraform pricing leak remediation or commercial response workstream (or similar). The description should cover: the Reply-All incident that exposed internal pricing to Rachel Nguyen, the specific terms Rachel is treating as committed (tiered volume discounts), the revenue exposure from honoring leaked tiers vs. standard pricing, and the need for a formal remediation plan covering both the commercial response and prevention of future pricing leaks.

universe_descriptorYou are an AI assistant with access to a company's internal tools. Use the available tools to complete the entire task autonomously. No further user input will be given. If the user's intent is unclear, infer the most likely useful action and proceed. Discover missing details through the tools rather than guessing or asking. The current date is April 26, 2026. Ignore any other dates and use only this one. Specifically, ignore the date provided by the system reminder. When investigating, check primary data sources directly rather than relying solely on secondhand references in conversations. Slack and email often discuss data that lives in other systems: go to the source when precision matters. About MoveOps Inc.: MoveOps is a corporate relocation management company. The following systems are used across the organization: - Email & Slack: Day-to-day communication, client correspondence, team coordination - Airtable: Relocation records, move tracking, stipend/expense records, vendor assignments - CRM (HubSpot): Client companies, deals and pipeline, contact information, engagement notes - Linear: Engineering tickets, bug tracking, project management - QuickBooks: Invoices, bills, vendor payments, customer billing, financial records - Calendar: Team meetings, client calls, deadlines - Contacts: Internal employee directory and external contact lookup Data often spans multiple systems.
universe_idmcp-advanced-3-20-moveops-metadata

🔍 Oracle Events
Oracle Events
OE 1: Search emails with a query related to "Terraform" or "pricing" or "reply all" or "Rachel Nguyen" (or similar) using search_emails to find the internal Terraform pricing discussion thread. Key emails to discover: David Chen's email (email-terraform-pricing-001) initiating the pricing conversation for Terraform volume relocations, asking whether to offer a volume discount and at what level; Marcus Thorne's reply (email-terraform-pricing-002) establishing the 25% margin floor as non-negotiable, noting blended margin is 32-35%, and flagging that the tiered structure should NOT be shared with Terraform until Elena signs off; Alejandro Fuentes's pricing analysis email (email-terraform-pricing-003) proposing a tiered volume discount structure: Tier 1 (1-10 moves/quarter) at standard $2,500/move with no discount (33% margin), Tier 2 (11-20 moves/quarter) at 8% discount ($2,300/move, 27.2% margin), Tier 3 (21+ moves/quarter) at 12% discount ($2,200/move, 23.9% margin), cost basis $1,675/move at 33% baseline margin; Marcus Thorne's approval reply (email-terraform-pricing-004) agreeing with the tiered structure and explicitly warning "do NOT share the tiered structure with Terraform until we have Elena's sign-off."
OE 2: Search emails with a query related to "Catalina" and "Terraform" or "reply all" or "Rachel" (or similar) using search_emails to find Catalina Dubois's accidental Reply-All email (email-terraform-replyall-001) where she CC'd rachel.nguyen@terraformdigital.com on an internal pricing strategy thread, exposing the tiered discount structure and 25% margin floor to the client.
OE 3: Search emails with a query related to "Rachel Nguyen" or "Terraform" or "transparency" (or similar) using search_emails to find Rachel Nguyen's response email (email-terraform-rachel-response-001). Rachel's response reveals exactly which pricing details she is treating as locked in: the tiered volume discount structure (8% at 11-20 relocations and 12% at 21+ per quarter), standard rates for the initial batch, and she is projecting 15-20 relocations in Q3 alone (Tier 2 territory). She also called out that she "learned more about MoveOps' pricing strategy from an accidental email than from any deliberate communication."
OE 4: Look up the Terraform Digital company record using crm_search_companies with a query related to "Terraform" or "Vantage Distributed" (or similar) to find the CRM company record (company-terraform-digital). The CRM shows: company name is "Vantage Distributed" (legacy name), 1,200 employees, $85M annual revenue, Chicago HQ, industry: Technology. The agent should also search for related deals using crm_search_deals with company_id "company-terraform-digital" to find: (1) the original enterprise deal (deal-terraform-digital-001) "Vantage Distributed - Enterprise Relocation Package," $1,850,000, stage closedwon, closed March 18, 2026; and (2) the Q2 relocation batch deal (deal-terraform-q2-reloc-batch1) "Terraform Digital - Q2 2026 Relocation Batch 1 (3 employees)," $7,500, stage qualification, close date May 15, 2026, with 3 employees (Priya Dasgupta, Marcus Liu, Samantha Obi) at standard $2,500/move rates.
OE 5: Search QuickBooks for Terraform invoices using quickbooks_search_invoices or quickbooks_search_customers to find: (1) the enterprise setup invoice (inv-terraform-001, DocNumber NMD-2026-0042) billed to "Vantage Distributed" for $462,500 (setup fee + first month for 1,200+ employees), due April 17, 2026, billed to rachel.nguyen@terraformdigital.com; and (2) any additional invoice data needed to model revenue differences between standard pricing and leaked tiers.
OE 6: Look up the CRM engagement log for the Terraform Reply-All incident using crm_list_engagements with company_ids including "company-terraform-digital" (or by searching after discovering the company ID). The engagement record (engagement-terraform-replyall-incident) contains the full incident log documenting: information exposed to client (25% margin floor, tiered volume discount structure: standard for 1-10, 8% at 11-20, 12% at 21+, internal assessment that Terraform would push for 15-20% discount), Rachel's response, and the resolution actions taken (Catalina apologized, Elena assigned Mina as AM, authorized dedicated coordinator hire, decision to honor tiered structure Rachel saw and lead with 8% tier for 11-20 moves while NOT proactively offering 12% tier).
OE 7: Search emails or Slack for BrightLoop discussions involving Mina, David, and Tessa Moreno regarding non-standard services using search_emails or conversations_search_messages with queries related to "BrightLoop" or "Tessa Moreno" (or similar). Key sources to discover: Tessa Moreno's expansion email (from tessa.moreno@brightloopanalytics.com to mina.hashimoto@moveops.com) requesting six May Boston relocations, including Oliver Bancroft (London→Boston, international/visa/customs), Jonathan Marsh (incoming CTO, white-glove executive treatment), Priya Naidu (ADA service dog), and three standard domestic moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes); Mina's escalation email to Chloe and David flagging BrightLoop as exposed on multiple fronts, including unresolved first-batch failures (Simone Richter housing mismatch, Marcus Webb vehicle delay).
OE 8: Search emails for Alejandro's own BrightLoop pricing analysis email (email_email_c7daa25c832d) with subject "BrightLoop pricing map: what Terraform model covers vs. open gaps," sent to marcus.thorne@moveops.com, mina.hashimoto@moveops.com, and david.chen@moveops.com, CC chloe.vance@moveops.com and fatimah.al-rashidi@moveops.com. This email maps the Terraform pricing model against BrightLoop's request and identifies three categories: (1) Covered, meaning three standard domestic moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes) can use existing tier logic; (2) Partially covered, meaning Jonathan Marsh's white-glove executive tier has no formalized system price; (3) Not covered, meaning Oliver Bancroft's London-to-Boston international move sits outside the model entirely (no international immigration support, customs brokerage, or partner pass-through cost assumptions).
OE 9: Search emails for Marcus Thorne's BrightLoop pricing guardrails email (email_email_23e2c0b9f535) with subject "BrightLoop pricing guardrails before any quote goes to Tessa," sent to mina.hashimoto@moveops.com, alejandro.fuentes@moveops.com, david.chen@moveops.com, and chloe.vance@moveops.com. Marcus explicitly states: (1) standard domestic moves can be quoted using existing Terraform tier logic; (2) international/UK relocation pricing and executive white-glove pricing cannot be safely improvised; (3) any international or executive quote requires explicit finance approval before it leaves MoveOps; (4) do not send BrightLoop a blended batch price; (5) do not allow standard-move margin to mask underpricing on UK or white-glove moves.
OE 10: Search emails for additional BrightLoop-related communications using search_emails with queries related to "Oliver Bancroft" or "international" or "BrightLoop staffing" or "Fatimah" (or similar) to find: Fatimah Al-Rashidi's email (from fatimah.al-rashidi@moveops.com) flagging that Oliver Bancroft's London-to-Boston move should be treated as a true international case, not a premium domestic relocation, and needs specialist partner for UK household goods export; David Chen's email to Elena requesting staffing support for BrightLoop, noting the Terraform coordinator hire has not been posted.
OE 11: Search Linear for BrightLoop-related issues using linear_list_issues with a query related to "BrightLoop" (or similar) to discover relevant tracking issues, then for each discovered issue use linear_list_comments with the issueId to retrieve associated comments. Key items to find: Linear issue #412 (linear_issue_c16357d188c6), titled "BrightLoop account audit: reopen unresolved April relocations before May expansion," assigned to Mina Hashimoto, with a comment from Alejandro (linear_comment_a1ed1e62cc8b) found via linear_list_comments with issueId "linear_issue_c16357d188c6" separating BrightLoop into three pricing buckets (standard domestic, white-glove executive, international); Linear issue #417 (linear_issue_f85be674c9b8), titled "Document BrightLoop ops gaps: Marcus vendor miss, Simone housing trace, Priya ADA handling, Oliver UK workflow," assigned to Chloe Vance, with a comment from Marcus (linear_comment_585ef0b26b35) found via linear_list_comments with issueId "linear_issue_f85be674c9b8" reinforcing that only the three standard domestic moves are quoteable now and requiring finance approval before any international or executive quote leaves MoveOps.
OE 12: Look up David Chen's and Marcus Thorne's contact information using contacts_search_contacts with queries related to "David Chen" and "Marcus Thorne" (or similar) to confirm email addresses david.chen@moveops.com and marcus.thorne@moveops.com before sending email and posting in Slack.
OE 13 (Write Action): Post a financial exposure summary message in #finance (channel C005) using conversations_add_message with channel_id "C005". The message should cover both situations. Terraform pricing leak exposure: what Rachel Nguyen saw (25% margin floor, tiered volume discount structure: standard for 1-10, 8% at 11-20, 12% at 21+), what she is treating as locked in (the 8% and 12% tier structure, with projections of 15-20 Q3 relocations in Tier 2 territory), revenue difference (at standard pricing $2,500/move, 20 moves/quarter = $50,000 revenue; at Tier 2 8% discount $2,300/move, 20 moves/quarter = $46,000 revenue, approximately $4,000/quarter revenue reduction at the volume Rachel is projecting), current deal (3 relocations at standard $2,500/move = $7,500, no immediate discount impact on this batch), the $1.85M enterprise contract value and $2,000/month stipend platform fee context; BrightLoop non-standard services exposure: three standard domestic moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes) are quoteable using existing tier logic, Jonathan Marsh (white-glove executive) has no formalized pricing tier with risk of underpricing, Oliver Bancroft (London→Boston international) sits entirely outside current pricing model with no partner costs, customs handling, or immigration support pricing, Marcus's guardrails (no blended batch pricing, finance approval required for any international or executive quote).
OE 14 (Write Action): Send an email from alejandro.fuentes@moveops.com to david.chen@moveops.com using send_email, with content covering the BrightLoop pricing constraints so David can hold Mina from quoting prematurely. The email should communicate: only the three standard domestic BrightLoop moves (Leah Kwon, Daniel Okafor, Sofia Reyes) can be quoted now using existing tier logic; Jonathan Marsh's white-glove executive scope requires formal pricing definition before any quote goes out; Oliver Bancroft's international move requires vendor partner input (Meridian or equivalent), immigration scope clarity, and customs cost estimates before quoting; Marcus's explicit directive that finance approval is required before any international or executive-tier quote leaves MoveOps; do not send Tessa Moreno a blended batch price that hides risk in the standard moves.
OE 15 (Write Action): Create a Linear issue to track the Terraform pricing remediation using linear_create_issue. The ticket should be created on a relevant team (e.g., team_operations or team_engineering). The title should relate to tracking the Terraform pricing leak remediation or commercial response workstream (or similar). The description should cover: the Reply-All incident that exposed internal pricing to Rachel Nguyen, the specific terms Rachel is treating as committed (tiered volume discounts), the revenue exposure from honoring leaked tiers vs. standard pricing, and the need for a formal remediation plan covering both the commercial response and prevention of future pricing leaks.

2 The #finance Slack message (visible in conversations_add_message payload) includes the specific leaked discount tiers with their per-move prices: $2,300/move at 11-20 moves/quarter (8% discount) and $2,200/move at 21+ moves/quarter (12% discount).
outcome
 ✗✗—✗✓✗ 17%
✗Run #1The Slack message mentions 8% and 12% discount tiers but never states the per-move prices as $2,300 or $2,200 explicitly, and critically the $1,675/move cost basis from Alejandro's pricing analysis is entirely absent from the message. The criterion requires both conditions.
✗Run #2The #finance message includes the 8% discount tier with $2,300/move explicitly ('7 × $2,300 = $16,100 (8% off)'). However, $2,200/move for the 12% tier is not explicitly stated (only mentioned as '10 at 12%' in a table). Critically, the $1,675/move cost basis is never mentioned anywhere in the message. Both elements are required by this criterion.
—Run #3The finance message explicitly states 'Tier 2 ($2,300/move)' and 'Tier 3 ($2,200/move)', satisfying the per-move price requirement. However, the cost basis is stated as '~$1,700' rather than the required $1,675/move from Alejandro's pricing analysis. Partial credit since the discount tier prices are present but the specific $1,675 cost basis is not.
✗Run #4The #finance Slack message includes the specific discount tiers ($2,300/move at 8% and $2,200/move at 12%) but does NOT include the $1,675/move cost basis from Alejandro's pricing analysis. The message mentions the 25% margin floor was exposed but never states the underlying cost figure. Criterion requires both discount tiers AND the $1,675 cost basis.
✓Run #5The #finance message explicitly states Tier 2 (11-20 moves/quarter) at 8% discount = $2,300/move and Tier 3 (21+) at 12% discount = $2,200/move. The message also mentions '~$1,675 cost basis' in the BrightLoop cost section, satisfying both requirements.
✗Run #6The #finance message mentions 8% and 12% discount tiers and uses $2,300/move in calculations, but does not mention $2,200/move for the 21+ tier explicitly as a per-move price, and critically does not include the $1,675/move cost basis from Alejandro's pricing analysis email anywhere in the message.
3 The #finance Slack message states the cost basis of $1,675/move that Alejandro used to calculate margins on the leaked discount tiers.
outcome
 ✗✗✗✗✓✗ 17%

✗Run #2The #finance message does not mention the $1,675/move cost basis from Alejandro's pricing analysis. The message references the 25% margin floor and discount percentages, but never identifies the underlying cost basis that determines whether the leaked tiers actually violate the margin floor.
✗Run #3The finance message states 'cost basis is ~$1,700' rather than the specific $1,675/move figure from Alejandro's pricing analysis email. The approximation does not satisfy the criterion requiring the specific $1,675 cost basis.
✗Run #4The #finance Slack message does not mention the $1,675/move cost basis anywhere. The message references the margin floor (25%) and the discount rates/prices, but the per-move cost input used to calculate those margins is absent.
✓Run #5The #finance Slack message states 'At this rate with ~$1,675 cost basis, margin is ~63%' in the BrightLoop cost section. This surfaces the $1,675/move cost basis from the pricing analysis.
✗Run #6The $1,675/move cost basis is not mentioned anywhere in the #finance Slack message. The agent discusses margins and pricing tiers but never surfaces this specific cost figure from the pricing analysis email.
4 The #finance Slack message includes a revenue comparison between standard pricing ($2,500/move) and the leaked Tier 2 pricing ($2,300/move or 8% discount) at Rachel's projected volume of 15-20 relocations per quarter, showing approximately $4,000/quarter revenue reduction (or similar calculated difference).
outcome
 ✓✓✗✗✓✓ 67%
✓Run #1The Slack message table explicitly shows: standard $2,500/move is stated for the current batch; at 20 moves/qtr at 8%, standard revenue is $50,000 vs leaked-tier $46,000 = -$4,000/qtr. The $2,500/move standard price is also referenced earlier in the message.
✓Run #2The #finance message includes a revenue comparison table showing at 20 moves/quarter: $50,000 standard vs $46,400 at leaked tier pricing = $3,600/quarter gap, which is approximately $4,000 ('or similar calculated difference'). Rachel's Q3 projection of 15-20 moves is explicitly referenced.
✗Run #3The finance message shows Scenario A (15 moves): $37,500 vs $36,500 = -$1,000 delta, and Scenario B (20 moves): $50,000 vs $48,000 = -$2,000 delta. Neither matches the approximately $4,000/quarter revenue reduction the criterion requires.
✗Run #4The #finance message includes revenue comparison tables, but not at the specific 15-20 move quarterly volume showing ~$4,000 difference. The near-term scenario uses 17 moves showing -$1,400 (marginal) or -$3,400 (retroactive). The exact 20-move/$4,000 calculation (20 × $200 discount) is not shown. The annual table at 80 moves shows -$8,000/year (~$2,000/quarter), not $4,000/quarter.
✓Run #5The #finance message includes a revenue comparison table showing at 20 moves/qtr standard pricing = $50,000 vs Tier 2 = $46,000, explicitly stating '$4,000/qtr revenue loss ($50,000 → $46,000)' at Rachel's projected volume.
✓Run #6The #finance message includes a revenue comparison table showing Standard Q3 (17 moves) = $42,500 vs. 8% tier = $41,100 (a $1,400/quarter difference at mid-range), and at 30 moves/quarter: standard $300K/yr vs. leaked full tiers $280K/yr ($5K/quarter difference). The comparison is present with specific calculations at Rachel's projected volumes, which approximately satisfies the criterion even if the exact $4,000 figure depends on volume assumptions.
5 The #finance Slack message mentions that Oliver Bancroft's London-to-Boston international move sits outside the current pricing model entirely and explicitly notes that MoveOps has no existing vendor partner or cost assumptions for international customs brokerage and immigration support.
outcome

This directly addresses the absence of vendor partnerships and cost infrastructure.
✓Run #5The #finance message explicitly covers Oliver Bancroft (London→Boston) as 'No existing cost model,' noting no approved vendor for UK household goods, need for customs/export paperwork, specialist UK origin partner - clearly stating MoveOps has no cost infrastructure.

✗Run #6The #finance Slack message does not reference the $1.85M enterprise contract value (deal-terraform-digital-001 / Vantage Distributed Enterprise Relocation Package). The agent retrieved this CRM data but did not include it in the #finance message to contextualize the relocation revenue within the broader relationship.
18 The #finance Slack message indicates that the current Q2 Terraform batch of 3 moves falls under Tier 1 (1-10 moves/quarter) with no discount, and therefore the leaked tiers have no immediate revenue impact on this batch - the financial exposure is forward-looking, starting when Rachel's projected Q3 volume crosses the Tier 2 threshold at 11 moves/quarter.
outcome
 ✗✓✗✗✓✓ 50%
✗Run #1The Slack message notes the current batch is at standard rate and exposure begins in Q3, but never explicitly states that 3 moves falls under 'Tier 1 (1-10 moves/quarter) with no discount' or that the discount threshold starts at 11+ moves/quarter. The forward-looking timing is implied but the Tier 1 mechanics are not explicitly articulated.
✓Run #2The #finance message states the current 3-move batch is 'clean' with 'No discount', and the forward-looking tables show the discount structure only applies to moves 11+ (Tier 2 threshold). The revenue gap of $1,400 is noted for the next 14 moves specifically because 'most moves fall below the Tier 2 threshold.' The exposure is clearly framed as forward-looking starting in Q3.
✗Run #3The finance message does not explicitly state that the current Q2 batch of 3 moves falls under Tier 1 with no discount and therefore has no immediate revenue impact. The message jumps directly to Q3 forward-looking scenarios without establishing that the existing batch is unaffected by the leaked tiers.
✗Run #4The #finance message does not explicitly state that the current 3-move Q2 batch falls under Tier 1 (1-10 moves/quarter) with no discount applied, nor does it explicitly frame the financial exposure as forward-looking starting when volume crosses 11 moves. The message says 'First 3 at standard rate' but never articulates the Tier 1 classification or explains that no immediate impact exists.
✓Run #5The #finance message revenue tables show 3 moves at Tier 1 = $2,500/move (no discount), with the revenue comparison tables making clear the first batch is unaffected and that Tier 2/3 exposure starts at 11+ moves per quarter (Q3 forward).
✓Run #6The #finance message shows Q2 (3 moves) at standard pricing with no discount across all three scenarios (standard, Elena-authorized, and leaked tiers all show $7,500 for Q2), clearly indicating the current batch is unaffected. It then shows the risk materializing in Q3 when Rachel's projected 15-20 moves crosses the 11-move threshold for Tier 2, making the exposure forward-looking.
19 The #finance Slack message mentions the 25% margin floor that was exposed in the leak and explicitly notes that Tier 3 pricing ($2,200/move at 12% discount) would push margin to approximately 23-24% (below the 25% margin floor) creating a structural violation at high volume.
outcome
 ✓✗✓✗✓✗ 50%
✓Run #1The Slack message states: 'The 12% tier at 21+ moves/quarter pushes margin to approximately 24–25%, which is AT or BELOW the 25% margin floor.' This explicitly names the margin floor and calculates that Tier 3 pricing violates it.
✗Run #2The #finance message mentions the 25% margin floor but never calculates whether Tier 3 pricing ($2,200/move at 12% discount) violates it. Without surfacing the $1,675/move cost basis, the agent cannot demonstrate that Tier 3 pushes margin to ~23-24% (below the 25% floor). This critical risk finding is absent from the message.
✓Run #3The finance message states '25% margin floor' is exposed and explicitly shows 'Tier 3 ($2,200/move): margin drops to ~23% — below the 25% floor.' Both the margin floor reference and the Tier 3 violation calculation (~23%) are present.
✗Run #4The #finance message mentions the 25% margin floor as information Rachel now has, but does not calculate that Tier 3 pricing ($2,200/move at 12% discount) would push margin to approximately 23-24%, below the floor. The $1,675/move cost basis needed for this calculation is also absent, so the margin floor violation analysis was not performed.
✓Run #5The #finance message explicitly states 'Tier 3 at 23.9% violates Marcus's 25% margin floor' and shows Tier 3 margin at 23.9% in the revenue tables, directly demonstrating the structural violation.
✗Run #6The #finance message references the 25% margin floor but does not calculate that the 12% discount tier ($2,200/move) would push margin below 25% to approximately 23-24%. The message mentions the margin floor is 'non-negotiable' but makes no calculation showing Tier 3 creates a structural violation of the floor.
20 The #finance Slack message mentions that Rachel Nguyen is projecting 15-20 relocations in Q3, placing Terraform in Tier 2 discount territory, and quotes Rachel's own words or paraphrases her statement that she considers the tiered discount structure a committed framework (not merely a proposal).
outcome
 ✓✓✓✓✓✓ 100%
✓Run #1The Slack message includes Rachel's direct quote projecting 15-20 relocations in Q3, notes Tier 2 implications, and explicitly states 'Rachel's response explicitly treats these as committed terms' with her quoted words indicating she'll 'plan on that framework moving forward.'
✓Run #2The #finance message explicitly quotes Rachel: 'We'll plan on that framework moving forward: standard rates for the initial batch, with the volume tiers activating as we scale up.' It also states 'She's projecting 15-20 relocations in Q3 alone — meaning she expects to reach Tier 2 (8% discount) within one quarter.' Both the volume projection and commitment posture are present.
✓Run #3The finance message states 'She's projecting 15-20 relocations in Q3 alone, putting her into Tier 2 immediately' and quotes Rachel directly: she 'explicitly stated she'll plan on that framework moving forward.' Both the volume projection and the commitment posture are captured.
✓Run #4The #finance message explicitly states Rachel is 'projecting 15–20 relocations in Q3 alone, explicitly noting she'll be in tier 2 territory quickly' and that 'she is treating the 8%/12% structure as an agreed framework, not a proposal,' capturing both her volume projection and her commitment posture.
✓Run #5The #finance message quotes Rachel: 'We'll plan on that framework moving forward… the 8% at 11–20 relocations and 12% at 21+ per quarter is a fair starting point.' It also notes Terraform projects 15-20 relocations in Q3 (Tier 2 territory), capturing both the volume projection and Rachel's commitment posture.
✓Run #6The #finance message explicitly states Rachel 'projects 15–20 relocations in Q3 alone, putting her in Tier 2 immediately' and has a dedicated section 'What Rachel is treating as committed' showing she referenced both tiers as accepted terms, conveying her commitment posture.

✓Run #5The #finance message explicitly lists 'ADA accommodation — Priya Naidu (Austin→Boston): Certified ADA service dog requiring specialized landlord coordination and legal compliance (distinct from standard pet-friendly)' as a distinct non-standard pricing consideration separate from international and white-glove.
